items.title: Pick the right implementation
items.prefer: `list` for stacks, `collections.deque` for queues, `heapq`/`PriorityQueue` for priority queues.
items.why: The wrong choice turns whole-program complexity from O(n) into O(n²).
items.example:
```python
from collections import deque
stack: list[str] = []
queue: deque[str] = deque()
```

items.title: Bound your queues
items.why: Backpressure surfaces problems early and protects memory.
items.example:
```python
import asyncio
buf = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=1000)
await buf.put(token)   # blocks producer when full
```

items.title: Use `nlargest`/`nsmallest` for top-k
items.avoid: Sorting the whole list and slicing the first k.
items.prefer: `heapq.nlargest(k, items, key=...)` — O(n log k) instead of O(n log n).
items.why: Cheaper time and memory when k ≪ n (common in retrieval).
items.example:
```python
import heapq
top = heapq.nlargest(10, chunks, key=lambda c: c.score)
```

items.title: Match the traversal to the ADT
items.avoid: Mixing recursion (implicit stack) with an explicit queue without noticing.
items.prefer: BFS — explicit deque + popleft; DFS — explicit list + pop (or recursion).
items.why: Keeps the traversal order intentional and predictable.
